era_context: The early 15th century is marked by the Ming Dynasty's consolidation under Emperor Yongle, the Ottoman Empire's expansion in the Balkans and Anatolia, and the Great Schism in the Catholic Church. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War continues between England and France, with factional conflict emerging in France between Burgundians and Armagnacs. The Portuguese Age of Exploration is beginning under Prince Henry the Navigator. In Asia, Zheng He's treasure voyages are underway, and the Ming Dynasty is at the height of its naval and diplomatic power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Pacific (sparse European records; Chinese sources may be underrepresented)
Indigenous Americas (reliance on post-conquest accounts and archaeology)

events.title: Assassination of Louis, Duke of Orléans
events.description: On 23 November 1407, Louis, Duke of Orléans, brother of King Charles VI of France, is assassinated in Paris by agents of John the Fearless, Duke of Burgundy. This murder is a pivotal moment in late medieval French history, triggering the Burgundian-Armagnac civil war that will dominate French politics for decades. The assassination weakens France during the Hundred Years' War and eventually leads to English military intervention and the Treaty of Troyes (1420). The murder is justified by John the Fearless as a necessary act against a corrupt court, but it is widely condemned and marks the beginning of factional conflict that will destabilize the French kingdom.
key_figures: John the Fearless, Duke of Burgundy (instigator)
King Charles VI of France (mentally unstable)

sources.name: French royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: The assassination is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles. The date of 23 November 1407 is firmly established in multiple sources.
cross_references: Contributes to English intervention in France (1415 onwards)

events.title: Zheng He's first treasure voyage continues through the Indian Ocean
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's first great treasure voyage, which departed from Nanjing in 1405, continues through the Indian Ocean in 1407. The massive fleet of over 300 ships and 27,000 men visits ports in Southeast Asia, India, and the Arabian Peninsula, establishing tributary relationships and collecting exotic goods and tribute for the Ming court. The voyage demonstrates Ming naval supremacy and diplomatic prestige, and it establishes trade networks and diplomatic contacts that will be maintained through subsequent voyages. The voyage is celebrated in Ming court records as a triumph of imperial power and Confucian virtue.
key_figures: Tributary rulers from Southeast Asia, India, and the Arabian Peninsula

sources.name: Luo Maodeng, Longjiang Baochuan (later compilation, 16th century)
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and port sites

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's first voyage is well-documented in Ming imperial records. The voyage's continuation through 1407 is established in contemporary sources.

events.title: Council of Constance is called to address the Great Schism
events.description: Pope Gregory XII calls for the convening of the Council of Constance to address the Great Schism in the Catholic Church. The council is formally convened in 1414, but preparations and negotiations begin in 1407. The council will eventually depose all three papal claimants and elect Martin V as the sole pope, ending the schism. The calling of the council represents a recognition that the Council of Pisa (1409) has failed to resolve the crisis and that a more authoritative council is needed.
key_figures: Emperor Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, patron of the council)

sources.name: Dietrich of Niem, De Schismate (contemporary chronicle)
Historiographic consensus on the Great Schism

events.certainty_note: The calling of the Council of Constance is documented, but the exact date of the initial call is somewhat uncertain. Preparations began in 1407, but the council was formally convened in 1414. The event is confirmed, but the year is approximate.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast continues
events.description: Under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, Portuguese expeditions continue to explore and map the West African coast. By 1407, Portuguese ships have established trading posts and forts along the Moroccan and Mauritanian coasts, seeking gold, spices, and enslaved people. These early voyages lay the groundwork for the Portuguese Age of Exploration and the eventual rounding of the Cape of Good Hope. The expeditions are motivated by economic gain, religious conversion, and geographic curiosity.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea (15th-century compilation)
Portuguese royal records and trading post documentation

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast is well-documented, but specific events in 1407 are less precisely dated. The general trajectory of exploration is confirmed; individual voyage dates may be approximate.
cross_references: Part of broader Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1500s)

disconfirming_evidence: The calling of the Council of Constance is sometimes dated to 1407, but the council was not formally convened until 1414. Preparations and negotiations began in 1407, but the actual council did not meet until later. The significance of the Council of Pisa (1409) is often overstated; it actually worsened the schism by creating three papal claimants instead of two. The Council of Constance (1414–1418) was the truly decisive council that resolved the schism. The 'Age of Exploration' framing applied to Portuguese activities in 1407 is somewhat anachronistic; these were primarily trading and raiding expeditions, not systematic exploration in the later sense.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1407 is moderate but geographically uneven. European events (assassination of Louis of Orléans, preparations for the Council of Constance) are well-recorded in contemporary chronicles and official records, with a Western institutional bias. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) provide detailed records of Zheng He's voyages. Portuguese sources are later compilations, introducing potential dating uncertainties. African and indigenous American history remains poorly documented for this year, relying on later accounts and archaeology. The historiographic tradition privileges European and East Asian sources, potentially marginalizing other regions.

graph_edges.note: Assassination of Louis of Orléans triggered the Burgundian-Armagnac conflict, which led to English intervention and the Treaty of Troyes
Continuation of first voyage in 1407 led to its return in 1408
Calling of Council of Constance in 1407 led to its formal convening in 1414
Portuguese exploration in 1407 is part of the broader Age of Exploration (15th–17th centuries)
